identifier.example:
// 👍
      identifier.verify('x') === 'x'
      identifier.verify('abc123') === 'abc123'
      identifier.verify('_123') === '_123'
      identifier.verify('a_b_c_1_2_3') === 'a_b_c_1_2_3'

      // 👎
      identifier.verify('123xyz');   // cannot start with digit
      identifier.verify('x-y');      // invalid chars
      identifier.verify('!@#$%^&*()=+');  // invalid chars
      identifier.verify('🤯');       // invalid chars
      identifier.verify(42);         // not a string

nanoid.example:
// 👍
      nanoid().verify('1-QskICa3CaPGcKuYYTm1') === '1-QskICa3CaPGcKuYYTm1'
      nanoid().verify('vA4mt7CUWnouU6jTGbMP_') === 'vA4mt7CUWnouU6jTGbMP_'
      nanoid({ size: 7 }).verify('yH8mx-7') === 'yH8mx-7'
      nanoid({ min: 7, max: 10 }).verify('yH8mx-7') === 'yH8mx-7'
      nanoid({ min: 7, max: 10 }).verify('yH8mx-7890') === 'yH8mx-7890'

      // 👎
      nanoid().verify('123E4567E89B12D3A456426614174000'); // too long
      nanoid().verify('abcdefghijkl');                     // too short
      nanoid().verify('$*&(#%*&(');                        // invalid chars

iso8601.example:
// 👍
      iso8601.verify('2020-06-01T12:00:00Z'); // new Date('2020-06-01T12:00:00Z')

      // 👎
      iso8601.verify('2020-06-01');  // throws
      iso8601.verify('hello');       // throws
      iso8601.verify(123);           // throws
      iso8601.verify(new Date());    // throws (does not accept dates)

datelike.example:
// 👍
      datelike.verify('2024-01-08T12:00:00Z'); // strings...
      datelike.verify(new Date());             // ...or Date instances

      // 👎
      datelike.verify('2020-06-01');  // throws
      datelike.verify('hello');       // throws
      datelike.verify(123);           // throws

set.markdown:
An alias of `setFromArray()`.

      ⚠️ **IMPORTANT!** This decoder will change its behavior in a future
      version! If you rely on this decoder, use `setFromArray()` instead.

jsonArray.example:
// 👍
      jsonArray.verify([]);                  // []
      jsonArray.verify([{ name: 'Amir' }]);  // [{ name: 'Amir' }]

      // 👎
      jsonArray.verify({});                 // throws
      jsonArray.verify({ name: 'Alice' });  // throws
      jsonArray.verify('hello');            // throws
      jsonArray.verify(null);               // throws

verify.example: For example, take this simple number decoder. ```ts // 👍 number.verify(123); // 123 number.verify(3.1415); // 3.1415 // 👎 number.verify('hello'); // throws // Decoding error: // "hello" // ^^^^^^^ Must be number ```

value.example: ```ts // 👍 number.value(3); // 3 string.value('hi'); // 'hi' // 👎 number.value('hi'); // undefined string.value(42); // undefined ``` > _**NOTE:** When you use this on `optional()` decoders, you cannot distinguish a rejected value from an accepted ``undefined`` input value._

decode.example: For example, take this simple "number" decoder. When given an number value, it will return an ok: true result. Otherwise, it will return an ok: false result with the original input value annotated. ```ts // 👍 number.decode(3); // { ok: true, value: 3 }; // 👎 number.decode('hi'); // { ok: false, error: { type: 'scalar', value: 'hi', text: 'Must be number' } } ```

pipe.markdown:
```tsx
    const decoder =
      string
        .transform((s) => s.split(',').map(Number))
        .pipe(array(positiveInteger));

    // 👍
    decoder.verify('7') === [7];
    decoder.verify('1,2,3') === [1, 2, 3];

    // 👎
    decoder.verify('1,-3')  // -3 is not positive
    decoder.verify('🚀');   // not a number
    decoder.verify('3.14'); // not a whole number
    decoder.verify(123);    // not a string
    decoder.verify(true);   // not a string
    decoder.verify(null);   // not a string
    ```

    #### Dynamic decoder selection with ``.pipe()``

    With `.pipe()` you can also dynamically select another decoder, based on dynamic runtime value.

    ```tsx
    string
      .transform((s) => s.split(',').map(Number))
      .pipe((tup) =>
        tup.length === 2
          ? point2d
          : tup.length === 3
            ? point3d
            : never('Invalid coordinate'),
      );
    ```

doc_strings.array: Accepts arrays of whatever the given decoder accepts.
doc_strings.nonEmptyArray: Like `array()`, but will reject arrays with 0 elements.
doc_strings.tuple:
Accepts a tuple (an array with exactly _n_ items) of values accepted by the
_n_ given decoders.
doc_strings.poja:
Accepts any array, but doesn't validate its items further.

"poja" means "plain old JavaScript array", a play on `pojo()`.
doc_strings.optional:
Accepts whatever the given decoder accepts, or `undefined`.

If a default value is explicitly provided, return that instead in the
`undefined` case.
doc_strings.nullable:
Accepts whatever the given decoder accepts, or `null`.

If a default value is explicitly provided, return that instead in the `null`
case.
doc_strings.nullish:
Accepts whatever the given decoder accepts, or `null`, or `undefined`.

If a default value is explicitly provided, return that instead in the
`null`/`undefined` case.
doc_strings.constant: Accepts only the given constant value.
doc_strings.always:
Accepts anything, completely ignores it, and always returns the provided
value instead.

This is useful to manually add extra fields to object decoders.
doc_strings.never:
Rejects all inputs, and always fails with the given error message. May be
useful for explicitly disallowing keys, or for testing purposes.
doc_strings.null_: Accepts and returns only the literal `null` value.
doc_strings.undefined_: Accepts and returns only the literal `undefined` value.
doc_strings.unknown:
Accepts anything and returns it unchanged.

Useful for situation in which you don't know or expect a specific type. Of
course, the downside is that you won't know the type of the value statically
and you'll have to further refine it yourself.
doc_strings.truthy: Accepts anything and will return its "truth" value. Will never reject.
doc_strings.record:
Accepts objects where all values match the given decoder, and returns the
result as a `Record<string, V>`.
doc_strings.setFromArray:
Similar to `array()`, but returns the result as an [ES6
Set](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/JavaScript/Reference/Global_Objects/Set).
doc_strings.mapping:
Similar to `record()`, but returns the result as a `Map<string, T>` (an [ES6
Map](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/JavaScript/Reference/Global_Objects/Map))
instead.
doc_strings.set:
Renamed to `setFromArray` to make room for a future `set()` decoder that
works differently.
doc_strings.iso8601:
Accepts [ISO8601](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ISO_8601)-formatted strings,
returns them as `Date` instances.

This is very useful for working with dates in APIs: serialize them as
`.toISOString()` when sending, decode them with `iso8601` when receiving.
doc_strings.datelike:
Accepts either a Date, or an ISO date string, returns a Date instance.
This is commonly useful to build decoders that can be reused to validate
object with Date instances as well as objects coming from JSON payloads.
doc_strings.jsonObject: Accepts objects that contain only valid JSON values.
doc_strings.jsonArray: Accepts arrays that contain only valid JSON values.
doc_strings.json:
Accepts any value that's a valid JSON value.

In other words: any value returned by `JSON.parse()` should decode without
failure.

```typescript
type JSONValue =
    | null
    | string
    | number
    | boolean
    | { [string]: JSONValue }
    | JSONValue[]
```
doc_strings.instanceOf: Accepts any value that is an ``instanceof`` the given class.
doc_strings.lazy:
Lazily evaluate the given decoder. This is useful to build self-referential
types for recursive data structures.
doc_strings.prep:
Pre-process the data input before passing it into the decoder. This gives
you the ability to arbitrarily customize the input on the fly before passing
it to the decoder. Of course, the input value at that point is still of
``unknown`` type, so you will have to deal with that accordingly.
doc_strings.anyNumber:
Accepts any valid ``number`` value.

This also accepts special values like `NaN` and `Infinity`. Unless you
want to deliberately accept those, you'll likely want to use the
`number` decoder instead.
doc_strings.number:
Accepts finite numbers (can be integer or float values). Values `NaN`,
or positive and negative `Infinity` will get rejected.
doc_strings.positiveNumber: Accepts only non-negative (zero or positive) finite numbers.
doc_strings.positiveInteger: Accepts only non-negative (zero or positive) finite whole numbers.
doc_strings.object:
Accepts objects with fields matching the given decoders. Extra fields that
exist on the input object are ignored and will not be returned.
doc_strings.exact:
Like `object()`, but will reject inputs that contain extra fields that are
not specified explicitly.
doc_strings.inexact:
Like `object()`, but will pass through any extra fields on the input object
unvalidated that will thus be of `unknown` type statically.
doc_strings.pojo:
Accepts any "plain old JavaScript object", but doesn't validate its keys or
values further.
doc_strings.regex: Accepts and returns strings that match the given regular expression.
doc_strings.startsWith: Accepts and returns strings that start with the given prefix.
doc_strings.endsWith: Accepts and returns strings that end with the given suffix.
doc_strings.nanoid:
Accepts and returns [nanoid](https://zelark.github.io/nano-id-cc) string
values. It assumes the default nanoid alphabet. If you're using a custom
alphabet, use `regex()` instead.
doc_strings.nonEmptyString: Like `string`, but will reject the empty string or strings containing only whitespace.
doc_strings.email:
Accepts and returns strings that are syntactically valid email addresses.
(This will not mean that the email address actually exist.)
doc_strings.url: Accepts strings that are valid URLs, returns the value as a URL instance.
doc_strings.httpsUrl:
Accepts strings that are valid URLs, but only HTTPS ones. Returns the value
as a URL instance.
doc_strings.identifier:
Accepts and returns strings that are valid identifiers in most programming
languages.
doc_strings.uuid:
Accepts strings that are valid
[UUIDs](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/universally_unique_identifier)
(universally unique identifier).
doc_strings.decimal:
Accepts and returns strings with decimal digits only (base-10).
To convert these to numbers, use the `numeric` decoder.
doc_strings.hexadecimal: Accepts and returns strings with hexadecimal digits only (base-16).
doc_strings.numeric:
Accepts valid numerical strings (in base-10) and returns them as a number.
To only accept numerical strings and keep them as string values, use the
`decimal` decoder.
doc_strings.either:
Accepts values accepted by any of the given decoders.

The decoders are tried on the input one by one, in the given order. The
first one that accepts the input "wins". If all decoders reject the input,
the input gets rejected.
doc_strings.oneOf:
Accepts any value that is strictly-equal (using `===`) to one of the
specified values.
doc_strings.enum_: Accepts and return an enum value.
doc_strings.taggedUnion:
If you are decoding tagged unions you may want to use the `taggedUnion()`
decoder instead of the general purpose `either()` decoder to get better
error messages and better performance.

This decoder is optimized for [tagged
unions](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tagged_union), i.e. a union of
objects where one field is used as the discriminator.

```ts
const A = object({ tag: constant('A'), foo: string });
const B = object({ tag: constant('B'), bar: number });

const AorB = taggedUnion('tag', { A, B });
//                        ^^^
```

Decoding now works in two steps:

1. Look at the `'tag'` field in the incoming object (this is the field
   that decides which decoder will be used)
2. If the value is `'A'`, then decoder `A` will be used. If it's `'B'`, then
   decoder `B` will be used. Otherwise, this will fail.

This is effectively equivalent to `either(A, B)`, but will provide better
error messages and is more performant at runtime because it doesn't have to
try all decoders one by one.
doc_strings.select:
Briefly peek at a runtime input using a "scout" decoder first, then decide
which decoder to run on the (original) input, based on the information that
the "scout" extracted.

It serves a similar purpose as `taggedUnion()`, but is a generalization that
works even if there isn't a single discriminator, or the discriminator isn't
a string.
doc_strings.define:
Defines a new `Decoder<T>`, by implementing a custom acceptance function.
The function receives three arguments:

1. `blob` - the raw/unknown input (aka your external data)
2. `ok` - Call `ok(value)` to accept the input and return ``value``
3. `err` - Call `err(message)` to reject the input with error ``message``

The expected return value should be a `DecodeResult<T>`, which can be
obtained by returning the result of calling the provided `ok` or `err`
helper functions. Please note that `ok()` and `err()` don't perform side
effects! You'll need to _return_ those values.
doc_strings.summarize:
Walks the annotation tree and emits the annotation's key path within the
object tree, and the message as a series of messages (array of strings).
doc_strings.ok: Create a new Result instance representing a successful computation.
doc_strings.err: Create a new Result instance representing a failed computation.
doc_strings.difference:
Subtract two sets. This is a polyfill until we have it natively
available, see https://tc39.es/proposal-set-methods/#sec-set.prototype.difference
doc_strings.isPojo: Is the given value a POJO (plain old JavaScript object)?
doc_strings.decode:
Verifies the untrusted/unknown input and either accepts or rejects it.

Contrasted with `.verify()`, calls to `.decode()` will never fail and
instead return a result type.
doc_strings.verify:
Verifies the untrusted/unknown input and either accepts or rejects it.
When accepted, returns a value of type `T`. Otherwise fail with
a runtime error.
doc_strings.value:
Verifies the untrusted/unknown input and either accepts or rejects it.
When accepted, returns the decoded `T` value directly. Otherwise returns
`undefined`.

Use this when you're not interested in programmatically handling the
error message.
doc_strings.transform:
Accepts any value the given decoder accepts, and on success, will call
the given function **on the decoded result**. If the transformation
function throws an error, the whole decoder will fail using the error
message as the failure reason.
doc_strings.refine:
Adds an extra predicate to a decoder. The new decoder is like the
original decoder, but only accepts values that also meet the
predicate.
doc_strings.refineType:
Cast the return type of this read-only decoder to a narrower type. This is
useful to return "branded" types. This method has no runtime effect.
doc_strings.then:
Send the output of the current decoder into another decoder or acceptance
function. The given acceptance function will receive the output of the
current decoder as its input.

> _**NOTE:** This is an advanced, low-level, API. It's not recommended
> to reach for this construct unless there is no other way. Most cases can
> be covered more elegantly by `.transform()`, `.refine()`, or `.pipe()`
> instead._
doc_strings.pipe:
Send the output of this decoder as input to another decoder.

This can be useful to validate the results of a transform, i.e.:

  string
    .transform((s) => s.split(','))
    .pipe(array(nonEmptyString))

You can also conditionally pipe:

  string.pipe((s) => s.startsWith('@') ? username : email)
doc_strings.reject:
Adds an extra predicate to a decoder. The new decoder is like the
original decoder, but only accepts values that aren't rejected by the
given function.

The given function can return `null` to accept the decoded value, or
return a specific error message to reject.

Unlike `.refine()`, you can use this function to return a dynamic error
message.
doc_strings.describe:
Uses the given decoder, but will use an alternative error message in
case it rejects. This can be used to simplify or shorten otherwise
long or low-level/technical errors.
